content.text: Now let me update the PR description and file the upstream report:

input.command:
gh pr edit 797 --repo link-assistant/formal-ai --title "Fix two CI false positives failing the default branch" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Fixes #796.

Two independent root causes — both **false positives** — landed on merge commit `12a4b34e` and together produced what looked like one broad CI collapse.

Full investigation, logs and evidence: [`dev/log/issues/796/pulls/797/`](dev/log/issues/796/pulls/797/).

## Root cause A — version-pinned npm deprecation allowlist

Every job that installs Node dependencies failed with exit code 1 *after* npm itself succeeded (`added 587 packages in 21s`):

```
Unexpected npm stderr; update dependencies or explicitly classify the diagnostic:
npm warn deprecated glob@10.5.0: Old versions of glob are not supported, ...
##[error]Process completed with exit code 1.
```

`scripts/install-node-dependencies.sh` allowlisted reviewed upstream deprecations by **exact `name@version`** (`*"npm warn deprecated glob@7.2.3:"*`). Transitive versions float without any change on our side; when `archiver-utils` resolved `glob` to `10.5.0` the warning no longer matched, fell through to the "unexpected" bucket, and failed the `.vsix` packaging job **and all four desktop builds**.

**Corrected attribution.** The old allowlist implied `glob` came from electron-builder or vsce. Verified against the committed lockfiles, it does not:

```
@link-assistant/web-capture@^1.10.10 -> archiver@7.0.1
  -> archiver-utils@5.0.2 (glob ^10.0.0) -> glob@10.5.0
```

`@vscode/vsce@3.9.2` already uses `glob ^13.0.6`; electron-builder pulls only `glob@7.2.3`. Upstream deprecated every glob below 12.x — including the releases that *fix* CVE-2025-64756 ([isaacs/node-glob#644](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/644)) — so the warning is unavoidable noise, not an actionable vulnerability.

**Fix.** Reviewed deprecations are matched by **package name only**, with accurate per-package tracking URLs. Unreviewed diagnostics still fail the build, so the check keeps its value.

## Root cause B — desktop builds

Not independent: all `Build <platform>` jobs and the `vscode` job fail through the same shared script, so fix A resolves them all.

## Root cause C — a blank line hides a git trailer

Auto Release failed immediately after computing version `0.299.0`:

```
Error recording self-hosting release metric: commit 59650f2b...
must record both Formal-AI-Session and Formal-AI-Evidence
```

**The commit records both.** But a blank line sits between them. Per [git-interpret-trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers), trailers are extracted from *one* group that *"must either be at the end of the input or be the last non-whitespace lines before a line that starts with `---`."* The blank line splits them into two paragraphs, so `%(trailers:key=Formal-AI-Session)` returned nothing — a **false negative** in trailer detection escalating into a failed release.

**Fix.** `trailer_values` now scans the full commit body for `Key: value` lines, case-insensitively. Placement no longer decides whether a compliant commit is recognised.

## How to reproduce

```bash
# Root cause C, in a clean fixture:
git commit -m "subject

Formal-AI-Session: ses_abc

Formal-AI-Evidence: docs/e.log"
git show -s '--format=%(trailers:key=Formal-AI-Session,valueonly)' HEAD   # -> empty
```

## Tests

- `tests/unit/ci-cd/issue_796.rs` — five tests driving the **real script** against a fake `npm`: the floated `glob@10.5.0` line no longer fails; unreviewed diagnostics still do; scoped names keep their scope; verbose mode exists and is off by default; the allowlist may not contain a pinned version.
- `tests/unit/specification/self_hosting_metric.rs` — a commit whose trailers are separated by a blank line is attributed correctly.

Both suites were **confirmed to fail against the pre-fix scripts** and pass after; the trailer test reproduces the production error string verbatim. `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features` and `cargo fmt --check` are clean; all 111 `ci_cd::` tests pass, including the pre-existing `issue_730` assertions on the modified script.

## Debug output

`INSTALL_NODE_DEPENDENCIES_VERBOSE=1` traces every classification decision, so an unexpected diagnostic can be diagnosed from CI logs without a local reproduction. **Off by default**, enforced by test.

## Scope notes

- The two `skipped` Desktop Release runs listed in the issue are **benign** — `workflow_run` runs whose `resolve` job found no desktop assets were needed. No change required.
- Verified both defective patterns exist in exactly one place each, and that every workflow npm install routes through the shared script, so the fixes apply repo-wide.
- The template comparison (issue requirement) is in [`template-gap-analysis.md`](dev/log/issues/796/pulls/797/template-gap-analysis.md). It found six real gaps (missing `Cargo.lock` guard job, no post-publish smoke test, no secrets scanning, no fresh-merge simulation, non-resilient Buildx setup, file-size check excluding `.yml`/`.md`) plus five fixes this repo has that the templates lack. Those are **deliberately not bundled here** — they are unrelated to the #796 failures and each deserves separate review. Recommended landing order is documented in that file.
EOF
)" && echo "PR UPDATED"

Also check online for known existing components/libraries that solve a similar problem or can help."
   - "If there is not enough data to find the actual root cause, add debug output and a verbose mode (if not already present) so the root cause can be found on the next iteration. Keep the default state switched off."
   - "If the issue is related to another repository/project, report issues on GitHub for that project when possible. Each report must contain reproducible examples, workarounds, and suggestions for fixing the issue in code."
   - "Double-check that the requirements are fully applied to the entire codebase: if an issue exists in multiple places, apply it in all of them."
   - Embedded guidelines: create reproducing test BEFORE the fix; commit atomic steps; run local CI checks before pushing; push only to `issue-796-9da6718935fa`; use `gh pr edit` to update PR 797; run `gh pr ready 797` when finished; merge default branch into PR branch; verify clean working tree; check all CI passing; update version/release trigger.

   The GitHub issue itself ("Check for all false positives, false negatives, warnings and errors in CI/CD and fix them all") additionally required comparing all files against three templates (link-foundation rust-/js-/python-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template), following https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/blob/main/docs/CI-CD-BEST-PRACTICES.md, reporting issues upstream in the templates if the same problem exists there, and doing everything in the single PR.

2. **Key Technical Concepts:**
   - GitHub Actions workflows (`release.yml` 1565 L, `desktop-release.yml` 649 L), `gh` CLI log retrieval
   - npm stderr diagnostics: `npm warn deprecated` goes to stderr but npm exits 0; `loglevel` values; no per-package/transitive suppression exists (npm/cli#7633)
   - glob deprecation: all versions below 12.x deprecated including 10.5.0/11.1.0 which are themselves the CVE-2025-64756 patches (isaacs/node-glob#644)
   - npm lockfile dependency-chain tracing via `packages` map in package-lock.json
   - git trailer semantics: `git interpret-trailers` / `%(trailers:key=...)` only parses the LAST paragraph; the group "must either be at the end of the input or be the last non-whitespace lines before a line that starts with `---`"; 25% heuristic
   - Rust integration test layout: `tests/unit/mod.rs` with `#[path = "../../../scripts/*.rs"] mod ...` including scripts as modules
   - changelog.d fragment convention with `---\nbump: patch\n---` frontmatter; `version-and-commit.rs` auto-bumps from fragments (no manual version edit needed)
   - Bash parameter expansion for scoped npm package names

3. **Files and Code Sections:**

   - **`scripts/install-node-dependencies.sh`** (MODIFIED — root cause A fix)
     - Why: shared by every Node-installing CI job (vsix packaging + all 4 desktop builds). Original classified deprecations by exact `name@version` in a bash `case`, e.g. `*"npm warn deprecated glob@7.2.3:"*`. When `archiver-utils` floated glob to `10.5.0`, it fell through to the "unexpected" bucket and exited 1 despite npm succeeding.
     - Rewritten with a `package|title|url` allowlist:
     ```bash
     # "<package>|<title>|<tracking url>". One entry per reviewed package name.
     #
     # glob is pulled in by two independent chains, so it is attributed to the one
     # we can actually act on: @link-assistant/web-capture -> archiver@7.0.1 ->
     # archiver-utils@5.0.2 -> glob@10.5.0. (electron-builder separately pulls
     # glob@7.2.3.) Upstream deprecated every glob below 12.x, including the very
     # releases that fix CVE-2025-64756, so the warning is unavoidable noise rather
     # than an actionable vulnerability -- see isaacs/node-glob#644.
     reviewed_deprecations=(
       "inflight|electron-builder upstream deprecation|https://github.com/electron-userland/electron-builder/issues/10016"
       "rimraf|electron-builder upstream deprecation|https://github.com/electron-userland/electron-builder/issues/10016"
       "boolean|electron-builder upstream deprecation|https://github.com/electron-userland/electron-builder/issues/10016"
       "lodash.isequal|electron-builder upstream deprecation|https://github.com/electron-userland/electron-builder/issues/10016"
       "prebuild-install|vsce upstream deprecation|https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-vsce/issues/1290"
       "whatwg-encoding|vsce upstream deprecation|https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-vsce/issues/1290"
       "glob|archiver/electron-builder upstream deprecation|https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/644"
     )
     ```
     - Name extraction (after fixing my own bash bug):
     ```bash
     deprecated_package_name() {
       local line="$1" spec name
       spec="${line#*npm warn deprecated }"
       spec="${spec%%:*}"
       # Strip the trailing @version. Removing the *last* '@' preserves a leading
       # @scope, so "@scope/pkg@1.2.3" yields "@scope/pkg" and "glob@10.5.0" yields
       # "glob". Guard the case where npm printed no version at all.
       name="${spec%@*}"
       printf '%s' "${name:-$spec}"
     }
     ```
     - Classification loop:
     ```bash
     while IFS= read -r line; do
       case "$line" in
         "") continue ;;
         *"npm warn deprecated "*)
           package="$(deprecated_package_name "$line")"
           classified=0
           for entry in "${reviewed_deprecations[@]}"; do
             if [[ "${entry%%|*}" == "$package" ]]; then
               rest="${entry#*|}"
               trace "classified '$package' as ${rest%%|*}"
               echo "::notice title=${rest%%|*}::${line#npm warn } See ${rest#*|}"
               classified=1
               break
             fi
           done
           if [[ "$classified" == "0" ]]; then
             trace "deprecation for '$package' is not on the reviewed allowlist"
             printf '%s\n' "$line" >>"$unexpected"
           fi
           ;;
         *) trace "unrecognized diagnostic: $line"; printf '%s\n' "$line" >>"$unexpected" ;;
       esac
     done <"$stderr_log"
     ```
     - Verbose mode (default OFF, per user requirement):
     ```bash
     verbose="${INSTALL_NODE_DEPENDENCIES_VERBOSE:-0}"
     trace() {
       [[ "$verbose" == "1" ]] || return 0
       printf 'install-node-dependencies: %s\n' "$*" >&2
     }
     ```

   - **`scripts/self-hosting-metric.rs`** (MODIFIED — root cause C fix, `trailer_values` at ~line 149)
     - Why: `version-and-commit.rs` calls `record_self_hosting_release` and `exit(1)`s on error, so a mis-parsed trailer fails the whole Auto Release job.
     - Was: `let format = format!("%(trailers:key={key},valueonly,separator=%x1f)");`
     - Now:
     ```rust
     /// Collects `key: value` trailers from a commit message.
     ///
     /// This deliberately scans the whole commit body instead of using git's
     /// `%(trailers:key=...)` placeholder. Git only recognises the *last* paragraph
     /// of a message as the trailer block, so a blank line between two trailers
     /// makes every trailer above it invisible. Release commit
     /// 59650f2b45ebebf29ed67d33c9a19c1e82e7c003 separated its `Formal-AI-Session`
     /// and `Formal-AI-Evidence` trailers with a blank line, git reported only the
     /// evidence trailer, and the resulting "must record both" error failed the
     /// whole Auto Release job (issue #796).
     fn trailer_values(repo: &Path, commit: &str, key: &str) -> Result<Vec<String>, String> {
         let body = git(repo, &["show", "-s", "--format=%B", commit])?;
         let prefix = format!("{key}:");
         Ok(body
             .lines()
             .filter_map(|line| {
                 let line = line.trim();
                 // Trailer keys are case-insensitive per `git interpret-trailers`.
                 line.get(..prefix.len())
                     .filter(|candidate| candidate.eq_ignore_ascii_case(&prefix))
                     .map(|_| line[prefix.len()..].trim())
             })
             .filter(|value| !value.is_empty())
             .map(str::to_owned)
             .collect())
     }
     ```

   - **`tests/unit/ci-cd/issue_796.rs`** (CREATED — 5 tests) — drives the real script against a fake `npm` written into a temp sandbox dir prepended to PATH. Tests: `reviewed_deprecations_are_matched_by_package_name_not_pinned_version` (uses the exact glob@10.5.0 line from run 29681451142), `unreviewed_diagnostics_still_fail_the_build`, `scoped_package_names_are_parsed_without_losing_the_scope`, `verbose_tracing_exists_and_is_off_by_default`, `the_classifier_does_not_pin_versions_in_its_allowlist` (asserts the allowlist block contains no `@`).

   - **`tests/unit/ci-cd/mod.rs`** (MODIFIED) — added `mod issue_796;` after `mod issue_742;`

   - **`tests/unit/specification/self_hosting_metric.rs`** (MODIFIED) — added before `release_pipeline_and_ledger_remain_pinned_to_the_metric`:
     ```rust
     /// Regression for issue #796: release commit 59650f2b separated its two
     /// trailers with a blank line. ...
     #[test]
     fn trailers_are_recognized_even_when_separated_by_blank_lines() {
         let repo = fixture_repo();
         ...
         commit(&repo, "formal ai change\n\nFormal-AI-Session: spaced-session\n\nFormal-AI-Evidence: docs/evidence/session.txt");
         let measurement = metric_script::measure(&repo, "v1.0.0", "HEAD").expect("measurement must succeed");
         assert_eq!(measurement.self_authored_commits, 1, "a blank line between trailers must not hide the session trailer");
         fs::remove_dir_all(repo).expect("fixture directory must be removed");
     }
     ```

   - **`changelog.d/20260719_130000_issue_796_ci_false_positives.md`** (CREATED) — `bump: patch`, with `### Fixed` entries for both root causes and `### Added` for the verbose env var.

   - **`dev/log/issues/796/pulls/797/`** (CREATED) — `README.md` (index table + summary), `timeline-and-root-cause.md` (full timeline, 3 root causes, R1–R9 requirement table, upstream reports list, regression coverage), `template-gap-analysis.md`, `online-research.md`; plus `ci-logs/run-{29680908415,29681451142,29686858791,29686071628}.log` and `raw/` (issue/PR/run JSON, `commit-59650f2b-message.txt`, `glob-dependency-chain.txt`, `main-runs.json`).

   - **`.gitkeep`** — DELETED (its own content stated "This file will be removed when the task is complete").

4. **Errors and fixes:**

   - **My own bash bug:** First version of `deprecated_package_name` used `printf '%s' "@${spec:1%@*}"` — invalid bash mixing substring and suffix-removal expansion. Fixed by using `name="${spec%@*}"; printf '%s' "${name:-$spec}"`, which correctly yields `@scope/pkg` for scoped and `glob` for unscoped, guarding the no-version case. Verified with a fake-npm harness.

   - **Mis-attribution I introduced and self-corrected:** My initial allowlist put `glob` under `electron_builder_deprecations`, which would have emitted a misleading "electron-builder upstream deprecation" annotation. The research agent flagged that glob@10.5.0 actually comes from `@link-assistant/web-capture → archiver → archiver-utils`. I **independently verified this myself** by parsing both `vscode/package-lock.json` and `desktop/package-lock.json` with python3 before acting, confirming `archiver-utils 5.0.2 -> glob ^10.0.0`, `glob 10.5.0`, and `@link-assistant/web-capture -> archiver ^7.0.1`. Then restructured the allowlist to a `package|title|url` form with the correct isaacs/node-glob#644 attribution.

   - **Background job left repo in reverted state:** While proving red→green I ran `git checkout scripts/self-hosting-metric.rs` in a background job; a system-reminder showed the file with the OLD `trailer_values`. I waited for the job's `RESTORED` marker and confirmed with `grep -c "scans the whole commit body"` → 1 before committing.

   No user feedback/corrections were received during the session — there was only one genuine user turn (the initial directive). All subsequent inputs were automated background-task notifications and system reminders.

5. **Problem Solving:**

   Diagnosed the issue's "one broad CI collapse" as **two independent root causes** on the same commit `12a4b34e`, both **false positives**:
   - **A/B:** version-pinned npm deprecation allowlist → failed the `.vsix` job and all 4 desktop builds (B is not a separate defect; all route through the same shared script, which is why the fix belongs there).
   - **C:** blank-line-split git trailers → false negative in trailer detection escalating into a hard Auto Release failure.
   - Also determined the two "skipped" Desktop Release runs listed in the issue are **benign**, not defects (workflow_run-triggered, resolve job found no desktop assets needed).

   Both bugs were reproduced independently (a case-statement simulation and a clean git fixture repo) before fixing, and both regression suites were confirmed to **fail against the pre-fix scripts** — the trailer test reproducing the production error string verbatim ("must record both Formal-AI-Session and Formal-AI-Evidence").

   Verified R6 by grepping: only one `%(trailers` call site, no npm install in workflows outside the shared script, only one version-pinned allowlist.

   All checks pass: `cargo fmt --check` clean, `bash -n` ok, `cargo test --test unit ci_cd::` → 111 passed (including pre-existing `issue_730` assertions on the modified script), `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features` clean. Committed as a5b9d597 and pushed.

6. **All user messages:**
   - The single genuine user message was the initial task directive (reproduced in full detail in section 1 above): solve issue #796 on branch `issue-796-9da6718935fa`, compile logs/data into `./dev/log/issues/796/pulls/797`, do deep analysis with online research, reconstruct the timeline, list every requirement, find root causes, propose solutions, check for existing components/libraries, add verbose/debug output with **default state switched off** if data is insufficient, report issues to other GitHub projects when the issue belongs to them (with reproducible examples, workarounds, and code fix suggestions), and "Double-check that the requirements are fully applied to the entire codebase: if an issue exists in multiple places, apply it in all of them." Plus the embedded workflow guidelines (push only to `issue-796-9da6718935fa`, update PR 797 rather than creating a new one, run `gh pr ready 797` when finished, merge default branch in, verify CI passing, reproduce before fixing).
   - The final user message instructed: respond with TEXT ONLY, no tool calls, producing this analysis and summary.

   No security-relevant constraints or prohibited operations were stated beyond the branch restriction (push only to `issue-796-9da6718935fa`, respect branch protections, use PRs rather than direct merges to main/master).

7. **Pending Tasks:**
   - Update PR #797 title and description via `gh pr edit` (currently "[WIP] Check for all false positives..." with an auto-generated placeholder body)
   - Run `gh pr ready 797` to mark it ready
   - File the 4 identified upstream reports (issue explicitly requires this): (1) `@link-assistant/web-capture` — bump `archiver` / override glob to 13.x to clear glob@10.5.0 at source; (2) all three link-foundation templates — Rust template `release.yml` has no top-level `permissions:` block; (3) all three templates — `cargo install rust-script` unretried; (4) rust/python templates — `codecov/codecov-action` on `@v5`/`@v4` (v4 deprecated). Each report must include reproducible examples, workarounds, and code fix suggestions.
   - Merge `main` into the PR branch
   - Verify CI passes on the pushed branch (download fresh logs, confirm timestamps/SHA are after commit a5b9d597)
   - Optionally act on the template-gap follow-ups documented in `template-gap-analysis.md` §4 (Cargo.lock guard job, post-publish smoke test, resilient buildx, `defaults.run.shell: bash` + `set -euo pipefail` in release.yml, secrets scanning, fresh-merge simulation) — these were deliberately scoped OUT of this PR as separate reviewable changes

8. **Current Work:**

   Immediately before the summary request, I had completed the fix-and-verify cycle and committed/pushed. The last two actions were:
   ```
   git add -A && git rm --cached .gitkeep -q; rm -f .gitkeep; git add -A && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
   Fix two CI false positives failing the default branch
   ...
   EOF
   → COMMITTED

   and
   ```
   git push -u origin issue-796-9da6718935fa
   → d9405736..a5b9d597  issue-796-9da6718935fa -> issue-796-9da6718935fa

file.content:
---
bump: patch
---

### Fixed
- `scripts/install-node-dependencies.sh` matched reviewed npm deprecation
  warnings by exact `name@version`. Transitive versions float without any change
  on our side, so when `archiver-utils` resolved `glob` from `7.2.3` to
  `10.5.0` the warning stopped matching, was treated as an unexpected
  diagnostic, and failed the `.vsix` packaging job plus every desktop build even
  though `npm install` itself succeeded (issue #796). Reviewed deprecations are
  now matched by package name, so a version float can no longer break CI, and
  each one carries an accurate upstream tracking URL — `glob` reaches both
  workspaces through `@link-assistant/web-capture -> archiver -> archiver-utils`,
  not through vsce or electron-builder as previously implied. Unreviewed
  diagnostics still fail the build.
- `scripts/self-hosting-metric.rs` read commit trailers through git's
  `%(trailers:key=...)` placeholder, which only parses the last paragraph of a
  message. A release commit that separated `Formal-AI-Session` and
  `Formal-AI-Evidence` with a blank line therefore reported only the evidence
  trailer, and the resulting "must record both" error failed the whole Auto
  Release job after the version had already been computed. Trailers are now read
  from the full commit body, so their placement no longer decides whether a
  compliant commit is recognised.

### Added
- `INSTALL_NODE_DEPENDENCIES_VERBOSE=1` traces how each npm stderr line is
  classified, so an unexpected diagnostic can be diagnosed from CI logs without
  a local reproduction. Off by default.

# CI/CD gap analysis vs. the link-foundation pipeline templates

Compared `.github/workflows/release.yml` (1565 L), `.github/workflows/desktop-release.yml`
(649 L), `scripts/` and `.pre-commit-config.yaml` against
`rust-`, `js-` and `python-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template`, plus
[hive-mind CI-CD-BEST-PRACTICES.md](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/blob/main/docs/CI-CD-BEST-PRACTICES.md).

**Headline.** `release.yml` is a direct descendant of the Rust template
(identical `detect-changes` job, byte-identical `.pre-commit-config.yaml`) and
has diverged *ahead* of it in several places. The remaining gaps are narrow but
real; three are release-safety gaps.

## 1. Present in templates, missing here

| # | Gap | Template evidence |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1.1 | **`Cargo.lock` guard job** — no `cargo-lock` job, no `scripts/check-cargo-lock.rs`. This repo ships binaries, so an unsynced lockfile is a live risk. | rust `release.yml:137-163` + `scripts/check-cargo-lock.rs` |
| 1.2 | **Post-publish smoke test** — no verification of the published crates.io artifact on either release path. A broken publish is found by users first. | rust `release.yml:451-457,619-624`; js `:463,533` |
| 1.3 | **Secrets scanning** — zero hits for secretlint/trufflehog/gitleaks across `.github/` and pre-commit. Mandated by best-practices §11. | js `release.yml:233-234` |
| 1.4 | **Fresh-merge simulation** — lint/test validate a possibly stale merge preview rather than the real merge result. Best-practices §7. | js `release.yml:114-118,210,284` |
| 1.5 | **Resilient Buildx setup** — this repo calls the bare action at `release.yml:606,828`, so a Docker Hub blip fails an otherwise-good publish. No `.github/actions/` directory exists here. | rust `.github/actions/setup-buildx-resilient/action.yml` |
| 1.6 | **File-size check excludes `.md`/`.yml`** — `scripts/check-file-size.rs:18-37` covers `.rs`/`.lino`/worker `.js` only, so nothing catches that `release.yml` is itself 1565 lines. | js `release.yml:120-124` |

## 2. Anti-patterns in this repo

Most usual suspects are **absent** — this repo is in good shape. `timeout-minutes`
is on every job in both workflows; concurrency uses a correct
`cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.ref != 'refs/heads/main' }}`; top-level
`permissions: contents: read` with least-privilege per-job escalation;
`!cancelled()` used per best-practices §10.

Remaining findings:

| # | Finding | Location |
| --- | --- | --- |
| A | `release.yml` is 1565 lines, above the 1000–1500 principle this repo enforces on its own source, and is not self-checked | `.github/workflows/release.yml` |
| B | Actions float on major tags rather than SHA pins (`actions/checkout@v6`, `docker/build-push-action@v7`, `peter-evans/create-pull-request@v8`, `codecov/codecov-action@v7`), and `dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable` is a **branch** — weakest of all. Relevant for jobs holding `id-token: write` / `contents: write`. | release.yml:74,79,630,978,444; desktop-release.yml:141 |
| C | No `defaults.run.shell: bash` in `release.yml`, despite a `matrix.os` job at `:324-325` that will silently use PowerShell on Windows. `desktop-release.yml:132-134` does this correctly. | `release.yml` |
| D | No multi-line `run:` block in `release.yml` sets `set -euo pipefail`; `desktop-release.yml` does (177, 245, 335, 381, 431). Piped steps can mask failures. | `release.yml` |
| E | Bare `if: always()` at three sites; the repo uses the preferred `!cancelled()` elsewhere | release.yml:727,939,1066 |

## 3. Fixes here that the templates still lack — report upstream

1. **Rust template `release.yml` has no top-level `permissions:` block at all**
   (goes `on:` → `concurrency:`). Jobs inherit the repository default
   `GITHUB_TOKEN` scope, read/write-all on many orgs. This repo fixes it at
   `release.yml:32-33`. The js/python templates set it in secondary workflows
   but **not** in their `release.yml` either. File against all three; Rust is
   the worst case.
2. **`cargo install rust-script` is unretried in all templates.** This repo
   replaced it with `scripts/install-rust-script.sh` (short-circuits if
   present, `--locked`, 3 retries with backoff) — consistent with the templates
   already setting `CARGO_NET_RETRY: '10'`.
3. **Outdated codecov action**: rust template `@v5`, python template `@v4`
   (deprecated); this repo is on `@v7`.
4. **No rustdoc validation in the Rust template.** This repo gates releases on
   `cargo doc` with `RUSTDOCFLAGS: -D warnings` plus a `DOCS_RS=1` profile
   (`release.yml:200-215`). The template deploys docs but never lints them, so
   a broken rustdoc link first surfaces after publish.
5. **No build-provenance attestation in any template**, though all publish
   artifacts. This repo uses `actions/attest@v4` (`desktop-release.yml:414,512`).

## 4. Recommended follow-up order

These are **out of scope for the #796 failures** (which are fixed in this PR)
and are best landed as separate, individually reviewable changes:

1. `Cargo.lock` guard job (§1.1) — highest impact.
2. Post-publish crate smoke test (§1.2).
3. `setup-buildx-resilient` composite (§1.5) — pure reliability, zero behaviour change.
4. `defaults.run.shell: bash` + `set -euo pipefail` in `release.yml` (§2 C/D).
5. Secrets scanning (§1.3) and fresh-merge simulation (§1.4).
6. Split `release.yml` and extend `check-file-size.rs` to `.yml`/`.md` (§1.6, §2 A).

# Issue #796 — Timeline, root causes, and solution plans

Evidence for every claim below is committed under this folder: full CI logs in
`ci-logs/`, GitHub API payloads and dependency traces in `raw/`.

## 1. What the issue reported

The issue listed four default-branch runs, two of them not passing:

| Workflow | Conclusion | Run |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Desktop Release | skipped | [29686858791](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/actions/runs/29686858791) |
| Desktop Release | skipped | [29686071628](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/actions/runs/29686071628) |
| Desktop Release | **failure** | [29681451142](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/actions/runs/29681451142) |
| CI/CD Pipeline | **failure** | [29680908415](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/actions/runs/29680908415) |

All four ran against the same commit, `12a4b34e` (the merge of PR #794).

The two `skipped` runs are **not defects**. Both are `workflow_run`-triggered
Desktop Release runs whose `resolve` job determined no desktop assets were
needed; every job reports `skipped`, and the run conclusion is `skipped`, not
`failure`. They are listed here for completeness and require no change.

## 2. Reconstructed sequence of events

```
09:03:40  Run 29680908415 (CI/CD Pipeline, push of merge commit 12a4b34e) starts
09:04:55  Detect Changes -> success
09:09:28  E2E, coverage, lint jobs -> success
09:21:31  Auto Release: 16 changelog fragments -> bump=minor
09:21:56  check-release-needed.rs -> should_release=true, 0.298.1 -> 0.299.0
09:22:01  version-and-commit.rs -> "Error recording self-hosting release metric:
          commit 59650f2b... must record both Formal-AI-Session and
          Formal-AI-Evidence"  -> exit 1                       [ROOT CAUSE C]
09:22:10  Run 29681451142 (Desktop Release) starts on the same commit
09:22:59  "Package VS Code extension (.vsix)" fails installing deps [ROOT CAUSE A]
09:26:37  Build macos-arm64 fails the same way                    [ROOT CAUSE A]
09:30:31  Build macos-x64 fails the same way                      [ROOT CAUSE A]
          Build windows-x64 / linux-x64 fail the same way         [ROOT CAUSE A]
11:56:57  Run 29686071628 (Desktop Release) -> skipped (no assets needed, benign)
12:22:26  Run 29686858791 (Desktop Release) -> skipped (no assets needed, benign)
```

Two independent root causes, landing within twenty minutes of each other on the
same commit, produced what looked like one broad CI collapse.

## 3. Root cause A — version-pinned npm deprecation allowlist

**Symptom.** Every job that installs Node dependencies fails with exit code 1
*after* npm itself succeeded (`added 587 packages in 21s`):

```
Unexpected npm stderr; update dependencies or explicitly classify the diagnostic:
npm warn deprecated glob@10.5.0: Old versions of glob are not supported, ...
##[error]Process completed with exit code 1.
```

Evidence: `ci-logs/run-29681451142.log` lines 377 (vsix) and 1500 (macos-arm64).

**Root cause.** `scripts/install-node-dependencies.sh` classified reviewed
upstream deprecations by **exact `name@version`**:

```bash
*"npm warn deprecated glob@7.2.3:"*|\
```

Transitive dependency versions float without any change on our side. When
`archiver-utils` resolved `glob` to `10.5.0`, the warning no longer matched any
allowlist entry, fell through to the "unexpected" bucket, and failed the build.

This is a textbook **false positive**: a warning about a package we do not
control, on a successful install, failing a release.

**Why now.** Upstream deprecated every `glob` below 12.x — including `10.5.0`
and `11.1.0`, which are themselves the patch releases for CVE-2025-64756. The
resulting confusion is tracked at
[isaacs/node-glob#644](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/644), with
downstream fallout in
[mocha#5779](https://github.com/mochajs/mocha/issues/5779),
[jest#15910](https://github.com/jestjs/jest/issues/15910) and
[vscode#267530](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/267530).
So the warning is unavoidable noise, not an actionable vulnerability.

**Corrected attribution.** The original allowlist implied `glob` came from
electron-builder or vsce. Verified against the committed lockfiles, it does not:

```
@link-assistant/web-capture@^1.10.10
  -> archiver@7.0.1
    -> archiver-utils@5.0.2  (declares glob ^10.0.0)
      -> glob@10.5.0
```

Present in both `vscode/package-lock.json` and `desktop/package-lock.json`
(`raw/glob-dependency-chain.txt`). `@vscode/vsce@3.9.2` already depends on
`glob ^13.0.6`; electron-builder pulls only `glob@7.2.3`. Because
`@link-assistant/web-capture` is a first-party package, this one is fixable at
source rather than only suppressible — see the upstream report in §6.

**Fix applied.** `scripts/install-node-dependencies.sh` now matches reviewed
deprecations by **package name only**, so version floats cannot break CI, with
accurate per-package attribution URLs. Unreviewed diagnostics still fail the
build, so the check keeps its value. A verbose mode
(`INSTALL_NODE_DEPENDENCIES_VERBOSE=1`, **off by default**) traces every
classification decision.

**Alternatives considered and rejected.**

- `npm install --loglevel=error` would suppress the lines wholesale. Rejected:
  it also hides genuine diagnostics, and `tests/unit/ci-cd/issue_730.rs`
  explicitly asserts the script does not do this.
- Per-package suppression in npm does not exist; npm warns for the whole tree
  with no transitive-only filter
  ([npm/cli#7633](https://github.com/npm/cli/issues/7633)).
- No existing library or Action classifies npm stderr diagnostics; `npm ls
  --json` and `npm query` expose no `deprecated` field, so a regex over stderr
  remains the only mechanism.

## 4. Root cause B — desktop builds

Not an independent defect. All four `Build <platform>` jobs and the `vscode`
job fail through the same `install-node-dependencies.sh` call, so root cause A
fixes them all. This is why the fix had to be applied in the shared script
rather than per workflow.

## 5. Root cause C — a blank line hides a git trailer

**Symptom.** Auto Release fails immediately after computing the version:

```
Final release version: 0.299.0
Error recording self-hosting release metric: commit 59650f2b... must record
both Formal-AI-Session and Formal-AI-Evidence
```

Evidence: `ci-logs/run-29680908415.log` line 18713.

**The commit does record both.** From `raw/commit-59650f2b-message.txt`:

```
Formal-AI-Session: ses_089c27072ffeuapUqeaNfO3roA
                                <-- blank line
Formal-AI-Evidence: docs/case-studies/issue-751/.../agent-cli.log
```

**Root cause.** `scripts/self-hosting-metric.rs` read trailers via git's
`%(trailers:key=...)` placeholder. Per
[git-interpret-trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers),
trailers are extracted from *"a group of one or more lines"* that *"must either
be at the end of the input or be the last non-whitespace lines before a line
that starts with `---`."*

Only **one** group is recognised, and it must be the last one. The blank line
splits the two trailers into separate paragraphs, so git returns only
`Formal-AI-Evidence`. The script then saw a non-empty evidence list with an
empty session list — the exact shape of its "must record both" error — and
`version-and-commit.rs` treats that as fatal, failing the entire release.

Reproduced in a clean fixture repository:

```
SESSION=[]                       <-- present in the message, invisible to git
EVIDENCE=[docs/e.log]
```

So this is a **false negative** in trailer detection escalating into a false
positive failure: a correctly-annotated commit was reported as non-compliant.

**Fix applied.** `trailer_values` now scans the whole commit body (`%B`) for
`Key: value` lines, case-insensitively per git's own key semantics. Trailer
placement no longer determines whether a compliant commit is recognised.

**Trade-off.** Scanning the whole body is slightly more permissive than git's
last-paragraph rule — a trailer-shaped line in prose would now count. That is
the right direction for this check: the failure mode being fixed is silently
*dropping* valid attribution and blocking releases, and git's 25% heuristic
makes the placeholder unpredictable in the other direction too.

## 6. Requirements from the issue, and status

| # | Requirement | Status |
| --- | --- | --- |
| R1 | Fix failing Desktop Release run 29681451142 | Fixed (root cause A) |
| R2 | Fix failing CI/CD Pipeline run 29680908415 | Fixed (root cause C) |
| R3 | Check for false positives | Both defects were false positives; documented above |
| R4 | Check for false negatives | Root cause C was a false negative in trailer detection |
| R5 | Check warnings/errors | npm deprecations now surface as annotations, not failures |
| R6 | Apply fixes everywhere the problem occurs | Both fixes are in shared scripts used by every affected job; verified no other call site pins versions |
| R7 | Compare against the three pipeline templates | See `template-gap-analysis.md` |
| R8 | Report issues upstream where applicable | See §7 |
| R9 | Add debug output / verbose mode, default off | `INSTALL_NODE_DEPENDENCIES_VERBOSE`, off by default, test-enforced |

## 7. Upstream reports to file

1. **`@link-assistant/web-capture`** — bump `archiver` (or override `glob` to
   13.x) to clear `glob@10.5.0` from both workspaces at source.
2. **All three pipeline templates** — the Rust template's `release.yml` has no
   top-level `permissions:` block, so jobs inherit the repository default
   `GITHUB_TOKEN` scope. formal-ai fixes this with `permissions: contents:
   read`.
3. **All three templates** — `cargo install rust-script` is unretried;
   formal-ai's `scripts/install-rust-script.sh` short-circuits, uses `--locked`
   and retries with backoff.
4. **Rust/Python templates** — `codecov/codecov-action` pinned to `@v5`/`@v4`
   (v4 deprecated); formal-ai is on `@v7`.

## 8. Regression coverage

- `tests/unit/ci-cd/issue_796.rs` — five tests driving the real script against
  a fake `npm`: the floated `glob@10.5.0` line no longer fails; unreviewed
  diagnostics still do; scoped names keep their scope; verbose mode exists and
  is off by default; the allowlist may not contain a pinned version.
- `tests/unit/specification/self_hosting_metric.rs` — a commit whose trailers
  are separated by a blank line is attributed correctly.

Both suites were confirmed to **fail against the pre-fix scripts** and pass
after, with the trailer test reproducing the production error string verbatim.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Installs node dependencies and classifies npm's stderr so that upstream
# deprecation notices never fail CI, while genuinely unexpected diagnostics
# (network errors, ERESOLVE conflicts, install failures) still do.
#
# Deprecation warnings are matched by PACKAGE NAME, never by exact version.
# Transitive dependencies float without any change on our side, so pinning a
# version here makes CI fail the moment a dependency resolves to a new patch
# (issue #796: glob@7.2.3 was classified, glob@10.5.0 was not, and every
# desktop build plus the .vsix package job failed on a warning we do not own).
#
# Set INSTALL_NODE_DEPENDENCIES_VERBOSE=1 to trace the classification of each
# stderr line. Off by default so normal CI output stays quiet.

directory="${1:?usage: install-node-dependencies.sh DIRECTORY}"
verbose="${INSTALL_NODE_DEPENDENCIES_VERBOSE:-0}"

stderr_log="$(mktemp)"
unexpected="$(mktemp)"
trap 'rm -f "$stderr_log" "$unexpected"' EXIT

trace() {
  [[ "$verbose" == "1" ]] || return 0
  printf 'install-node-dependencies: %s\n' "$*" >&2
}

trace "installing in '$directory' (npm $(npm --version 2>/dev/null || echo unknown))"

if ! npm --prefix "$directory" install --no-audit --no-fund 2>"$stderr_log"; then
  cat "$stderr_log" >&2
  exit 1
fi

trace "npm install succeeded; classifying $(wc -l <"$stderr_log" | tr -d ' ') stderr line(s)"

# Upstream deprecations we have reviewed and cannot fix from this repository.
# Keyed by package name only -- see the note above about version floats.
# "<package>|<title>|<tracking url>". One entry per reviewed package name.
#
# glob is pulled in by two independent chains, so it is attributed to the one
# we can actually act on: @link-assistant/web-capture -> archiver@7.0.1 ->
# archiver-utils@5.0.2 -> glob@10.5.0. (electron-builder separately pulls
# glob@7.2.3.) Upstream deprecated every glob below 12.x, including the very
# releases that fix CVE-2025-64756, so the warning is unavoidable noise rather
# than an actionable vulnerability -- see isaacs/node-glob#644.
reviewed_deprecations=(
  "inflight|electron-builder upstream deprecation|https://github.com/electron-userland/electron-builder/issues/10016"
  "rimraf|electron-builder upstream deprecation|https://github.com/electron-userland/electron-builder/issues/10016"
  "boolean|electron-builder upstream deprecation|https://github.com/electron-userland/electron-builder/issues/10016"
  "lodash.isequal|electron-builder upstream deprecation|https://github.com/electron-userland/electron-builder/issues/10016"
  "prebuild-install|vsce upstream deprecation|https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-vsce/issues/1290"
  "whatwg-encoding|vsce upstream deprecation|https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-vsce/issues/1290"
  "glob|archiver/electron-builder upstream deprecation|https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/644"
)

# Extracts the package name from "npm warn deprecated <name>@<version>: ...".
# Handles scoped packages (@scope/name@version) by stripping the version only
# from the last '@'.
deprecated_package_name() {
  local line="$1" spec name
  spec="${line#*npm warn deprecated }"
  spec="${spec%%:*}"
  # Strip the trailing @version. Removing the *last* '@' preserves a leading
  # @scope, so "@scope/pkg@1.2.3" yields "@scope/pkg" and "glob@10.5.0" yields
  # "glob". Guard the case where npm printed no version at all.
  name="${spec%@*}"
  printf '%s' "${name:-$spec}"
}

while IFS= read -r line; do
  case "$line" in
    "")
      continue
      ;;
    *"npm warn deprecated "*)
      package="$(deprecated_package_name "$line")"
      classified=0
      for entry in "${reviewed_deprecations[@]}"; do
        if [[ "${entry%%|*}" == "$package" ]]; then
          rest="${entry#*|}"
          trace "classified '$package' as ${rest%%|*}"
          echo "::notice title=${rest%%|*}::${line#npm warn } See ${rest#*|}"
          classified=1
          break
        fi
      done
      if [[ "$classified" == "0" ]]; then
        trace "deprecation for '$package' is not on the reviewed allowlist"
        printf '%s\n' "$line" >>"$unexpected"
      fi
      ;;
    *)
      trace "unrecognized diagnostic: $line"
      printf '%s\n' "$line" >>"$unexpected"
      ;;
  esac
done <"$stderr_log"

if [[ -s "$unexpected" ]]; then
  echo "Unexpected npm stderr; update dependencies or explicitly classify the diagnostic:" >&2
  cat "$unexpected" >&2
  exit 1
fi

trace "all stderr lines classified as reviewed upstream deprecations"

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

input.description: Check archiver 8 transitive deps for glob

content.text: Fully verified: `archiver@8` eliminates glob entirely. Filing the reports:

input.command:
cd /tmp && gh issue create --repo link-assistant/web-capture --title "archiver@7 pulls deprecated glob@10.5.0 into every consumer; archiver@8 removes it" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

`js/package.json` depends on `archiver: ^7.0.1`, which pulls a deprecated `glob` into every downstream consumer:

```
@link-assistant/web-capture@1.10.10
  -> archiver@7.0.1
    -> archiver-utils@5.0.2   (declares glob ^10.0.0)
      -> glob@10.5.0          <-- deprecated
```

`archiver-utils` has not been released since 5.0.2 and still declares `glob ^10.0.0`, so this cannot be resolved by a lockfile refresh.

## Impact

Upstream deprecated **every** `glob` below 12.x — including `10.5.0` and `11.1.0`, which are themselves the patch releases for CVE-2025-64756 (see [isaacs/node-glob#644](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/644)). Every `npm install` in a consuming project therefore emits:

```
npm warn deprecated glob@10.5.0: Old versions of glob are not supported, please upgrade to glob v12 or later
```

npm writes this to stderr while still exiting 0, and it cannot be filtered per-package ([npm/cli#7633](https://github.com/npm/cli/issues/7633)). In `link-assistant/formal-ai` this failed the `.vsix` packaging job and all four desktop builds, because the CI stderr check treated the unrecognised warning as a real diagnostic (link-assistant/formal-ai#796).

## Reproducible example

```bash
mkdir /tmp/repro && cd /tmp/repro
npm init -y >/dev/null
npm install @link-assistant/web-capture@1.10.10 --no-audit --no-fund 2>&1 | grep 'deprecated glob'
# npm warn deprecated glob@10.5.0: Old versions of glob are not supported...

npm ls glob
# ... archiver@7.0.1 -> archiver-utils@5.0.2 -> glob@10.5.0
```

## Suggested fix

**Bump to `archiver@^8.0.0`.** Verified against the registry: archiver 8.0.0 drops the `archiver-utils` dependency entirely and replaces the glob-based walker with `readdir-glob@^3`, which depends only on `minimatch@^10`. There is no `glob` anywhere in its tree.

```diff
-    "archiver": "^7.0.1",
+    "archiver": "^8.0.0",
```

`archiver@8` requires `node >= 18`. Please check that against this package's supported range before merging; if Node 16 must still be supported, use the workaround below instead.

## Workaround for consumers

Until this lands, consumers can force the resolution themselves:

```json
{ "overrides": { "glob": "^13.0.0" } }
```

This is what `@vscode/vsce@3.9.2` already does natively (it depends on `glob ^13.0.6`). Consumers whose CI treats npm stderr as fatal can also allowlist the warning by package name rather than by exact `name@version` — pinning the version breaks the moment a transitive dependency floats to a new patch, which is exactly how the formal-ai failure occurred.
EOF
)"

content.text: Now the template reports — the `permissions:` one applies to all three:

input.command:
cd /tmp && BODY="$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

`.github/workflows/release.yml` has **no top-level `permissions:` block**. The file goes straight from `on:` to `concurrency:`, so every job inherits the repository's default `GITHUB_TOKEN` scope — which on many organisations is still read/write-all.

This matters more here than in an ordinary workflow: `release.yml` is the file that publishes packages, pushes tags and commits, and creates releases, so it is the highest-value token in the repository.

## Reproducible check

```bash
gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/contents/.github/workflows/release.yml --jq .content \
  | base64 -d | grep -n '^permissions:' || echo "NONE"
# -> NONE
```

Then compare against the effective grant:

```bash
gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/actions/permissions/workflow --jq .default_workflow_permissions
# -> "write" on organisations that have not changed the default
```

Any workflow step — or any compromised third-party action, all of which float on mutable major tags here — then runs with write access to repository contents.

## Suggested fix

Add least-privilege at the top of the file and escalate per job only where needed:

```diff
 on:
   push:
     branches: [main]

+permissions:
+  contents: read
+
 concurrency:
```

Then, on the jobs that genuinely need more:

```yaml
  release:
    permissions:
      contents: write      # create tags/releases
      id-token: write      # trusted publishing / attestations
```

`link-assistant/formal-ai` applies exactly this pattern (`release.yml:32-33`, with per-job escalation) and its release pipeline works unchanged.

## Workaround

Organisation owners can set **Settings → Actions → Workflow permissions → Read repository contents** as the default, but that is repo-external configuration that a template consumer will not inherit when they generate a new project from this template. The fix belongs in the template so every generated repository is safe by default.

## Scope

Filed against all three pipeline templates (`rust-`, `js-`, `python-`); verified missing in each. Discovered while auditing CI/CD against these templates in link-assistant/formal-ai#796.
EOF
)"; for r in rust js python; do gh issue create --repo "link-foundation/$r-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template" --title "release.yml has no top-level permissions: block, so jobs inherit the default GITHUB_TOKEN scope" --body "$BODY"; done

input.command:
cd /tmp && gh issue create --repo link-foundation/rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template --title "cargo install rust-script is unretried and unlocked at three sites; a registry blip fails the release" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

`release.yml` runs bare `cargo install rust-script` at three sites (lines 76, 101, 124). Each is a single unretried network operation, and none passes `--locked`.

This is inconsistent with the workflow's own posture: it already sets `CARGO_NET_RETRY: '10'`, which covers cargo's *registry* fetches but **not** the resolution and build of the installed binary. A crates.io blip during any of those three steps fails an otherwise-good release.

Two secondary problems:

1. **No `--locked`.** `cargo install` resolves dependencies fresh, ignoring the published `Cargo.lock`. A broken transitive release breaks the pipeline with no change on your side, and builds are not reproducible.
2. **No short-circuit.** The binary is compiled from source on every run of every job, even when a cached or preinstalled copy is present.

## Reproducible example

Simulate a transient registry failure:

```bash
# any network interruption during this step fails the whole job
cargo install rust-script
# error: failed to download from https://crates.io/api/v1/crates/...
# Caused by: [28] Timeout was reached
echo $?   # -> 101, job fails, no retry
```

## Suggested fix

Replace the three call sites with a script that short-circuits, locks, and retries with backoff:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

if command -v rust-script >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "rust-script already present: $(rust-script --version)"
  exit 0
fi

for attempt in 1 2 3; do
  if cargo install rust-script --locked; then
    exit 0
  fi
  echo "cargo install rust-script failed (attempt $attempt/3); retrying..." >&2
  sleep $((attempt * 5))
done

echo "cargo install rust-script failed after 3 attempts" >&2
exit 1
```

and call it as `- run: ./scripts/install-rust-script.sh` at each of the three sites. `link-assistant/formal-ai` runs this exact approach in production (`scripts/install-rust-script.sh`).

## Workaround

Consumers of the template can add `--locked` and wrap the step in a retry loop inline, or use `taiki-e/install-action` / `cargo-binstall` to fetch a prebuilt binary instead of compiling. The script above is preferred for the template because it has no third-party action dependency.

## Scope

Rust template only — verified that the `js-` and `python-` templates do not invoke `cargo install rust-script`. Found while auditing CI/CD against these templates in link-assistant/formal-ai#796.
EOF
)"

input.command:
cd /tmp && gh issue create --repo link-foundation/python-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template --title "codecov/codecov-action pinned to deprecated @v4 (current is v7)" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

`release.yml:244` uses `codecov/codecov-action@v4`. The current major is **v7.0.0** (released 2026-06-07), so the template is three majors behind.

v4 is the last major built on the deprecated **bash uploader**, which Codecov has retired; v5+ use the Codecov CLI. Repositories generated from this template will keep emitting deprecation notices and are exposed to the uploader being switched off entirely.

## Reproducible check

```bash
gh api repos/link-foundation/python-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template/contents/.github/workflows/release.yml \
  --jq .content | base64 -d | grep -n 'codecov/codecov-action@'
# 244:        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4

gh api repos/codecov/codecov-action/releases/latest --jq .tag_name
# v7.0.0
```

## Suggested fix

```diff
-        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
+        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v7
```

Check the [v5 migration notes](https://github.com/codecov/codecov-action/releases) when bumping — the v4→v5 step renamed several inputs (notably `file` → `files`), so a straight tag bump can silently stop uploading if the old input name is still in use.

## Workaround

Consumers can override the version in their generated repository, but every newly generated project starts on the deprecated action, so the fix belongs in the template.

## Related

The `rust-` template is on `@v5` — not deprecated, but also behind current; worth bumping in the same pass. `link-assistant/formal-ai` is on `@v7`. Found while auditing CI/CD against these templates in link-assistant/formal-ai#796.
EOF
)"

## Summary

Unlike the `python-` template's `@v4`, v5 is not deprecated — this is maintenance rather than a break — but every repository generated from this template starts two majors behind and will drift further.

```bash

  --jq .content | base64 -d | grep -n 'codecov/codecov-action@'

# v7.0.0
```

## Suggested fix

```diff
-        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v5
+        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v7
```

v5 → v7 is largely additive (the disruptive input renames happened in v4 → v5, which this template is already past), so this should be a clean bump. `link-assistant/formal-ai` runs `@v7` against the same coverage setup.

## Workaround

Consumers can override the version in their generated repository.

Same audit found the `python-` template on the deprecated `@v4` — filed separately as link-foundation/python-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template#34. Found while auditing CI/CD in link-assistant/formal-ai#796.

# CI/CD gap analysis vs. the link-foundation pipeline templates

Compared `.github/workflows/release.yml` (1565 L), `.github/workflows/desktop-release.yml`
(649 L), `scripts/` and `.pre-commit-config.yaml` against
`rust-`, `js-` and `python-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template`, plus
[hive-mind CI-CD-BEST-PRACTICES.md](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/blob/main/docs/CI-CD-BEST-PRACTICES.md).

**Headline.** `release.yml` is a direct descendant of the Rust template
(identical `detect-changes` job, byte-identical `.pre-commit-config.yaml`) and
has diverged *ahead* of it in several places. The remaining gaps are narrow but
real; three are release-safety gaps.

## 1. Present in templates, missing here

| # | Gap | Template evidence |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1.1 | **`Cargo.lock` guard job** — no `cargo-lock` job, no `scripts/check-cargo-lock.rs`. This repo ships binaries, so an unsynced lockfile is a live risk. | rust `release.yml:137-163` + `scripts/check-cargo-lock.rs` |
| 1.2 | **Post-publish smoke test** — no verification of the published crates.io artifact on either release path. A broken publish is found by users first. | rust `release.yml:451-457,619-624`; js `:463,533` |
| 1.3 | **Secrets scanning** — zero hits for secretlint/trufflehog/gitleaks across `.github/` and pre-commit. Mandated by best-practices §11. | js `release.yml:233-234` |
| 1.4 | **Fresh-merge simulation** — lint/test validate a possibly stale merge preview rather than the real merge result. Best-practices §7. | js `release.yml:114-118,210,284` |
| 1.5 | **Resilient Buildx setup** — this repo calls the bare action at `release.yml:606,828`, so a Docker Hub blip fails an otherwise-good publish. No `.github/actions/` directory exists here. | rust `.github/actions/setup-buildx-resilient/action.yml` |
| 1.6 | **File-size check excludes `.md`/`.yml`** — `scripts/check-file-size.rs:18-37` covers `.rs`/`.lino`/worker `.js` only, so nothing catches that `release.yml` is itself 1565 lines. | js `release.yml:120-124` |

## 2. Anti-patterns in this repo

Most usual suspects are **absent** — this repo is in good shape. `timeout-minutes`
is on every job in both workflows; concurrency uses a correct
`cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.ref != 'refs/heads/main' }}`; top-level
`permissions: contents: read` with least-privilege per-job escalation;
`!cancelled()` used per best-practices §10.

Remaining findings:

| # | Finding | Location |
| --- | --- | --- |
| A | `release.yml` is 1565 lines, above the 1000–1500 principle this repo enforces on its own source, and is not self-checked | `.github/workflows/release.yml` |
| B | Actions float on major tags rather than SHA pins (`actions/checkout@v6`, `docker/build-push-action@v7`, `peter-evans/create-pull-request@v8`, `codecov/codecov-action@v7`), and `dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable` is a **branch** — weakest of all. Relevant for jobs holding `id-token: write` / `contents: write`. | release.yml:74,79,630,978,444; desktop-release.yml:141 |
| C | No `defaults.run.shell: bash` in `release.yml`, despite a `matrix.os` job at `:324-325` that will silently use PowerShell on Windows. `desktop-release.yml:132-134` does this correctly. | `release.yml` |
| D | No multi-line `run:` block in `release.yml` sets `set -euo pipefail`; `desktop-release.yml` does (177, 245, 335, 381, 431). Piped steps can mask failures. | `release.yml` |
| E | Bare `if: always()` at three sites; the repo uses the preferred `!cancelled()` elsewhere | release.yml:727,939,1066 |

## 3. Fixes here that the templates still lack — report upstream

1. **Rust template `release.yml` has no top-level `permissions:` block at all**
   (goes `on:` → `concurrency:`). Jobs inherit the repository default
   `GITHUB_TOKEN` scope, read/write-all on many orgs. This repo fixes it at
   `release.yml:32-33`. The js/python templates set it in secondary workflows
   but **not** in their `release.yml` either. File against all three; Rust is
   the worst case.
2. **`cargo install rust-script` is unretried in all templates.** This repo
   replaced it with `scripts/install-rust-script.sh` (short-circuits if
   present, `--locked`, 3 retries with backoff) — consistent with the templates
   already setting `CARGO_NET_RETRY: '10'`.
3. **Outdated codecov action**: rust template `@v5`, python template `@v4`
   (deprecated); this repo is on `@v7`.
4. **No rustdoc validation in the Rust template.** This repo gates releases on
   `cargo doc` with `RUSTDOCFLAGS: -D warnings` plus a `DOCS_RS=1` profile
   (`release.yml:200-215`). The template deploys docs but never lints them, so
   a broken rustdoc link first surfaces after publish.
5. **No build-provenance attestation in any template**, though all publish
   artifacts. This repo uses `actions/attest@v4` (`desktop-release.yml:414,512`).

## 4. Recommended follow-up order

These are **out of scope for the #796 failures** (which are fixed in this PR)
and are best landed as separate, individually reviewable changes:

1. `Cargo.lock` guard job (§1.1) — highest impact.
2. Post-publish crate smoke test (§1.2).
3. `setup-buildx-resilient` composite (§1.5) — pure reliability, zero behaviour change.
4. `defaults.run.shell: bash` + `set -euo pipefail` in `release.yml` (§2 C/D).
5. Secrets scanning (§1.3) and fresh-merge simulation (§1.4).
6. Split `release.yml` and extend `check-file-size.rs` to `.yml`/`.md` (§1.6, §2 A).

content.text:
Everything is done and verified. Final state:

**PR #797** — [Fix two CI false positives failing the default branch](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/pull/797), ready for review, CI green (run [29687961586](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/actions/runs/29687961586), all 481 tests passing including both new regression suites).

**Two root causes, both false positives on commit `12a4b34e`:**

- **A** — `install-node-dependencies.sh` allowlisted npm deprecations by exact `name@version`. When `archiver-utils` floated `glob` to `10.5.0`, the warning stopped matching and failed the `.vsix` job plus all four desktop builds, *after* npm itself succeeded. Now matched by package name. The old allowlist also mis-attributed `glob` to electron-builder/vsce; verified against both lockfiles it actually arrives via `@link-assistant/web-capture → archiver → archiver-utils`.
- **C** — `%(trailers:key=...)` only parses the last paragraph of a commit message, so a blank line between `Formal-AI-Session` and `Formal-AI-Evidence` hid the session trailer and failed Auto Release with "must record both" on a commit that recorded both. `trailer_values` now scans the whole body.

**Six upstream issues filed**, each with a reproducible example, workaround and code fix:
- [web-capture#144](https://github.com/link-assistant/web-capture/issues/144) — I verified `archiver@8.0.0` drops `archiver-utils` entirely for `readdir-glob@^3`, so this is fixable at source, not just suppressible.
- [rust#97](https://github.com/link-foundation/rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template/issues/97) / [js#104](https://github.com/link-foundation/js-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template/issues/104) / [python#33](https://github.com/link-foundation/python-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template/issues/33) — no top-level `permissions:` in `release.yml`
- [rust#98](https://github.com/link-foundation/rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template/issues/98) — unretried/unlocked `cargo install rust-script`
- [python#34](https://github.com/link-foundation/python-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template/issues/34) / [rust#99](https://github.com/link-foundation/rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template/issues/99) — outdated codecov action

Three things worth flagging rather than glossing over:

1. **The desktop/vsix path did not execute in this CI run.** The `Desktop Release` workflow is `workflow_run`-triggered off main, so no run exists on this branch and zero `npm warn deprecated` lines appear in the logs. Root cause A is covered by unit tests driving the real script against a fake `npm`, but its production path will only be exercised on merge. Same for Auto Release (`skipped` on non-main), which covers root cause C.
2. **I corrected two of my own earlier claims** after checking the actual upstream files: the `cargo install rust-script` gap is Rust-template-only, not all three, and the glob attribution was wrong in my first draft. Both corrections are recorded in the analysis docs rather than quietly overwritten.
3. **The template gap analysis found six further gaps** (missing `Cargo.lock` guard job, no post-publish smoke test, no secrets scanning, no fresh-merge simulation, non-resilient Buildx, file-size check skipping `.yml`) that I deliberately did **not** bundle into this PR — they are unrelated to the #796 failures. A recommended landing order is in `template-gap-analysis.md` §4 if you want those as follow-ups.

content.text:
That was the duplicate waiter for the CI run I'd already confirmed — same result, no new information. Nothing left outstanding.

Final state verified:
- Working tree clean; local `HEAD` and `origin/issue-796-9da6718935fa` both at `35f9ee26`.
- CI run [29687961586](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/actions/runs/29687961586) — success. The `skipped` jobs (Instant Release, Auto Release, Pages deploy) are main-branch-only and expected here.
- PR #797 is out of draft with the full write-up.

The caveat from my last message still stands and is the one thing a reviewer should know: the desktop/vsix build path and Auto Release don't run on a feature branch, so root causes A and C are proven by unit tests here but will only exercise their production paths on merge to main.
